text: The robotic cart is positioned and docked from the left shoulder side of the patient at an acute angle relative to the long axis of the surgical table, preparing for the esophagectomy procedure.
The camera port is positioned approximately two intercostal spaces below the tip of the right scapula, with the midline of the spine marked for reference.

The surgeon places R1 port seven centimeters from the midline, two to three intercostal spaces above the camera port, aligning parallel to the medial border of the scapula. R2 port is positioned two spaces below the camera port, also seven centimeters from the midline.
The assistant port is positioned in a triangulated manner between the camera port and robotic arm two, optimizing access for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon uses hot shears on the right side and a vessel sealer on the left to approach the infra-azygous part of the esophagus.
The surgeon begins the dissection by incising the inferior pulmonary ligament, allowing the lung to be retracted downward. The esophagus is then carefully separated from the underlying pericardium.
The surgeon approaches the cranial end and dissects the subcarinal group of lymph nodes, known as station VII, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.
After removing the dissected group of lymph nodes, the left main bronchus becomes visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the esophagus from the underlying pericardium, maintaining separation between these structures.
The surgeon employs the 'hanging from roof' technique, keeping the esophagus attached to the roof to facilitate dissection of the esophagus and thoracic duct.
In the caudal aspect of the surgical field, the vagus nerve is clearly visualized as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon demonstrates the prophylactic ligation of the thoracic duct to prevent iatrogenic injury during the dissection of more extensive esophageal lesions.
The surgeon exposes the left pleura, opposite to the previously visualized structures, during the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection caudally towards the lower gastroesophageal junction, bringing the left crus of the diaphragm into view.
The surgeon performs dissection of the vagal nerve branches below the tracheal bifurcation, ensuring the proprioceptive branches are preserved to facilitate better cough reflex postoperatively.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the left bronchial artery in the surgical field.
The surgeon proceeds with dissection in the supra azygous region, focusing on the anatomical structures above the azygous vein.
The surgeon meticulously dissects the right and left recurrent laryngeal nerves, carefully removing the lymph nodal packet surrounding these nerves in the mediastinum.
The surgeon carefully dissects the right recurrent laryngeal group of lymph nodes, taking care to avoid iatrogenic injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerves.
The surgeon exposes the membranous trachea during further dissection, using hot shears with caution to prevent iatrogenic injury to the delicate tracheal tissue.
The assistant uses the port to retract the trachea, visibly moving it away from the surgical field to prevent injury during the procedure.
The surgeon continues cranial dissection, revealing the subclavian vein in the surgical field.
The surgeon ligates the azygous vein, a routine step in the esophagectomy procedure, ensuring the vein is securely tied off to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon frees the esophagus from the thoracic inlet down to the hiatus, carefully dissecting the surrounding tissues.
